---
title: ""
pagetitle: "Target DAGs · from causal attribution to intervention"
description: "An application of the ACIC Causal SHAP argument: move from predictive credit to defensible intervention targets."
page-layout: full
toc: false
---

::: {.editorial-shell}
::: {.hero}
<p class="lineage-tag"><a href="https://www.tao-rwd.com/acic-2026/causal-shap">ACIC 2026 CAUSAL SHAP</a> <span>→</span> TARGET DAGS</p>

# SHAP has short memory. **Causal SHAP remembers the DAG.**

<p class="hero-lede">Target DAGs begins where that demonstration leaves off: once attribution respects the system, <mark>which upstream node should we actually try to change?</mark></p>

This project separates five questions that are too often collapsed into one—
from what predicts an outcome to what action is feasible, effective, and worth
testing.

<div class="hero-actions">
  <a class="button button-primary" href="#hierarchy">See the hierarchy</a>
  <a class="button button-secondary" href="https://www.tao-rwd.com/acic-2026/causal-shap">Begin with the ACIC project ↗</a>
</div>
:::

<figure class="archive-illustration wizard-illustration">
  <img
    src="https://www.tao-rwd.com/img/acic-2026/WizardOfShap.png"
    alt="A Wizard of SHAP hides severity of illness behind a curtain and attributes importance to vasopressors; Causal SHAP pulls back the curtain to reveal the upstream cause.">
  <figcaption><strong>The original ACIC motivation.</strong> A model can reward the closest measured feature while hiding the system that produced it. Illustration from the <a href="https://www.tao-rwd.com/acic-2026/causal-shap">archived project</a>.</figcaption>
</figure>

<div class="thesis-line" aria-label="From model explanation to intervention recommendation">
  <span>model explanation</span><b>→</b><span>causal attribution</span><b>→</b><span>intervention target</span><b>→</b><span>recommendation candidate</span>
</div>

## Prediction forgets what intervention remembers {#motivation}

<p class="section-deck">The inherited failure mode is simple: an outcome-near mediator can screen off its ancestors for prediction while continuing to transmit their causal effects.</p>

<div class="archive-comparison" aria-label="Causal truth compared with what ordinary SHAP sees">
  <figure>
    <p class="figure-kicker teal-ink">CAUSAL TRUTH</p>
    <img
      src="https://www.tao-rwd.com/img/acic-2026/SimpleTruth.jpg"
      alt="A hand-drawn causal chain in which cause A points to mediator B, which points to outcome Y.">
    <figcaption>A acts through B.</figcaption>
  </figure>
  <figure>
    <p class="figure-kicker orange-ink">WHAT ORDINARY SHAP CAN SEE</p>
    <img
      src="https://www.tao-rwd.com/img/acic-2026/SimpleWhatSHapSees.jpg"
      alt="The same chain with upstream cause A faded and mediator B enlarged because the mediator absorbs the predictive credit.">
    <figcaption>B absorbs A's predictive credit.</figcaption>
  </figure>
</div>

::: {.motivation-grid}
::: {.lineage-card}
<p class="section-number">THE HANDOFF</p>

### From coherent attribution to an actionable target.

The [ACIC project](https://www.tao-rwd.com/acic-2026/causal-shap) showed why
causal order and expert knowledge belong in the explanation. Target DAGs makes
the next test explicit: compare predictive attribution, causal ordering, and
**intervention propagation** against known total-effect truth.

The question is no longer only “who deserves credit?” It is “which upstream
node can change the downstream outcome?”
:::

::: {.markov-card}
<p class="hand-label orange-ink">why the last-node splat is expected</p>

<div class="chain" role="img" aria-label="Causal chain from X through mediator M to outcome Y">
  <span class="node cause">X</span><i>→</i><span class="node mediator">M</span><i>→</i><span class="node outcome">Y</span>
</div>

For the chain $X\rightarrow M\rightarrow Y$,

$$Y\perp X\mid M.$$

Once the model sees $M$, the ancestor $X$ adds no predictive information. Yet
if $M=aX+\varepsilon_M$ and $Y=bM+\varepsilon_Y$, its intervention effect is

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\,\mathbb E[Y\mid do(X=x)]=ab.$$

The mediator screens off $X$ for prediction while still transmitting its
effect. This is an estimand mismatch, not a failure of the Shapley axioms.
:::
:::

## One hierarchy. Five different questions. {#hierarchy}

<p class="section-deck">Each rung inherits the one before it but makes a stricter claim. A score from a lower rung cannot answer a higher-rung question.</p>

```{=html}
<ol class="hierarchy" aria-label="Hierarchy from prediction to recommendation">
  <li>
    <span class="rung-number">01</span>
    <div><p>Prediction</p><h3>What is likely to happen?</h3><small>A fitted model estimates an outcome from observed features.</small></div>
    <b class="rung-limit">association</b>
  </li>
  <li>
    <span class="rung-number">02</span>
    <div><p>Predictive attribution</p><h3>What did this model use?</h3><small>Ordinary SHAP allocates credit for the fitted prediction—not for changing the world.</small></div>
    <b class="rung-limit">model credit</b>
  </li>
  <li class="rung-current">
    <span class="rung-number">03</span>
    <div><p>Structural attribution</p><h3>What propagates under intervention?</h3><small>Set a DAG node, regenerate its descendants, and measure the full downstream response.</small></div>
    <b class="rung-limit">current evidence</b>
  </li>
  <li>
    <span class="rung-number">04</span>
    <div><p>Intervention target</p><h3>What can we actually change?</h3><small>Filter upstream influence through mutability, timing, constraints, and domain review.</small></div>
    <b class="rung-limit">next layer</b>
  </li>
  <li>
    <span class="rung-number">05</span>
    <div><p>Recommendation candidate</p><h3>Which feasible action is worth testing?</h3><small>Compare expected benefit, cost, and uncertainty. This is a decision claim, not another importance score.</small></div>
    <b class="rung-limit">decision layer</b>
  </li>
</ol>
```

<figure class="sketch-slot hierarchy-sketch" role="img" aria-label="Placeholder for a hand-drawn staircase from predictive explanation to an upstream intervention lever">
  <p class="slot-label">HAND-DRAWN INSERT 01 · THE TARGET DAG HIERARCHY</p>
  <div class="step-sketch" aria-hidden="true"><span>predict</span><span>explain</span><span>propagate</span><span>filter</span><span>recommend</span></div>
  <figcaption>Draw the SHAP splat on the lower steps; let a hand reach the upstream lever only after propagation and feasibility. Current evidence stops at step three.</figcaption>
</figure>

## The smallest useful demonstration {#demonstration}

<p class="section-deck">One controlled failure, one matched negative result, and one promising structural result. Everything else stays in the research record.</p>

```{=html}
<div class="result-grid">
  <article class="result-card result-warning">
    <p>Teaching stress test</p>
    <strong>46%</strong>
    <h3>Ordinary SHAP mass on ClinicVisit</h3>
    <small>The downstream proxy ranks first while its known total effect is <b>0%</b>.</small>
  </article>
  <article class="result-card">
    <p>NASA-topology simulation</p>
    <strong>τ 0.528</strong>
    <h3>DAG ordering alone</h3>
    <small>Essentially tied with matched ordinary SHAP at τ 0.506. Ordering is not propagation.</small>
  </article>
  <article class="result-card result-positive">
    <p>Structural prototype</p>
    <strong>τ 0.794</strong>
    <h3>Intervention propagation</h3>
    <small>Top-five recovery is 1.00 against frozen total-effect truth; promising, still provisional.</small>
  </article>
</div>
```

::: {.demo-dag}
::: {.demo-dag-copy}
<p class="section-number">THE TRAP, READ LEFT TO RIGHT</p>

### A useful lever can disappear behind a nearby measurement.

Diet and climate act upstream; hydration transmits part of their effect; the
outcome then predicts a clinic visit. A predictive explainer can faithfully
reward the final measurement even though intervening on it cannot change the
outcome that preceded it.
:::

<div class="wide-dag" role="img" aria-label="Diet and climate point to hydration, which points to outcome, which points to clinic visit">
  <div><span class="node cause">Diet</span><span class="node cause">Climate</span></div><i>→</i><span class="node mediator">Hydration</span><i>→</i><span class="node outcome">Outcome</span><i>→</i><span class="node proxy">Clinic visit</span>
</div>
:::

## The destination is a decision {#recommendation}

::: {.decision-band}
::: {.decision-copy}
<p class="section-number section-number-light">THE ELEVATOR PITCH</p>

### SHAP explains the model's ears. Target DAGs look for the system's levers.

Structural attribution is an input to a recommendation—not the recommendation
itself. The eventual target is a feasible action with positive propagated
benefit, acceptable cost, and robustness to uncertainty.
:::

::: {.decision-formula}
$$a^*=\arg\max_{a\in\mathcal A_{\mathrm{feasible}}}
\frac{\mathbb E[Y\mid do(a)]-\mathbb E[Y]}{C(a)}$$

$$\Pr\{\Delta_Y(a)>0\}\ge 1-\alpha$$

<p>effective × feasible × robust ÷ costly</p>
:::
:::

## What this project can claim {#status}

::: {.status-grid}
::: {.status-column}
<span class="status-tag established">ESTABLISHED HERE</span>

- A zero-effect downstream proxy can dominate ordinary SHAP in a known toy SCM.
- On the NASA-topology simulation, ordering-only and ordinary SHAP are tied.
- Structural propagation moves attribution toward frozen intervention truth.
:::

::: {.status-column}
<span class="status-tag provisional">NOT ESTABLISHED YET</span>

- NASA supplies topology, not empirical coefficients or effects.
- The structural result still needs larger runs and paired uncertainty.
- No attribution score in this repository is a clinical recommendation.
:::
:::

<div class="source-strip">
  <div><p class="section-number">ONE PAGE FOR THE ARGUMENT; ONE RECORD FOR THE DETAIL</p><strong>Methods, references, provenance, limitations, and reproducibility stay in <code>docs/</code>.</strong></div>
  <a class="button button-primary" href="https://github.com/andystats/causal-shap-target-dags/tree/main/docs">Open the research record</a>
  <a class="button button-secondary" href="https://github.com/andystats/causal-shap-target-dags">View the repository</a>
</div>
:::
